item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like how this is a closed case. Investigation and experiments were done until everything about the anomaly is explained.

Although I don't see what is stopping Michael from just turning himself in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a resource page for the fancy box stuff you use in this article?

item.title: Re: Nice.
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but doesn't the whole thing prevent movement? The Class-D test where half of their body was submerged they weren't able to move their legs.

item.title: Re: Nice.
item.content:encoded: Nah, I'd say it's like, softer ice. You know when some grocery store pudding gets a skin or film on top? Yeah, that's the pool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very familiar with the real-world Calvin University (live nearby, family members are alumni) and that meant some details, from the final interview in particular, were immersion-breaking.

"you were getting laid with half the cheerleading team" — Calvin's culture is super-Christian and super-conservative, as is the majority of the student body. Premarital sex is a huge no-no in the code of conduct, so this is… improbable.

"In a couple years, he would have been a serious Olympic contender. He would have jeopardized my chances of going to the Olympics." — Calvin is an NCAA Division III school that's mainly known for their basketball program. It would be incredibly, incredibly rare for even one college swimmer with realistic aspirations of the Olympics to choose to attend Calvin, let alone two at the same time as mentioned in this story.

So I guess, why Calvin? The story is fine, but the setting just doesn't make sense for the details of the plot and the motive for the murder. Why not set the story at a DI school with a high-end swim team? (Stanford, Cal, Michigan, Arizona State, to name a few)

item.title: Nice.
item.content:encoded: So uh, its basically harder version of ice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-Vote from me.

It's a good concept, well executed, however, addendum 4 ruins it for me. it feels like something is missing before the interview.

One minor criticism also is the use of Class-F amnestics as most sources say that those amnestics are for complete personality rewrites and is a very complex procedure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The intrigue of "what the hell is that water" works well, format is interesting and I absolutely love the last bit about crushing the perpetrator with guilt. On another note, thanks to them killing our SCP-source we're one superhuman SCP less, we now have supernatural pool water! That's some good alchemy. (+1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if you dove in the water, but the water never let go?

Cool premise that was well-executed. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it was mainly to show that it didn't do over the top things like cutting people in half, which I fully expected to happen to the D-Class. I guess that's why the Foundation does experiments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is good. I’m not at all familiar with the Alchemy Division canon or its characters, but not only did I love this article anyway, it’s really making me want to check the canon out. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It bothers me that this scientific document, in the thrid addendum, has "150kg WEIGHT". But then again, its Alchemic Town.

+1 anyways, nice skip and I enjoyed the format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure which I found more thematically satisfying - the casual 'we're not the cops, you have no rights' or the 'we're not going to punish you, you just have to live with your guilt'.

And the poor bastard can't really tell anyone about this encounter, now can he?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 3 gave us basically no new information. Yep, the surface tension sure didn't break. We knew that from the description, and a bunch of logs restating that is redundant. It's really unnecessary, takes away from the slightly less clinical tone of this article, and makes it drag on. The only purpose I could see in including it is padding between #2 and #4 so the reveal isn't so sudden.

The rest of the article is alright, and would probably get a +1 from me if the above issues weren't in the way.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was not aware of that, apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is entirely in character for both Agent Denton and Agent McDowell.

They're established characters in the Alchemy Canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-vote from me on this.

I like the idea, and the inclusion of the Foundations own Alchemical Division, but the interview at the end just seemed, weird? Like it was a different tone then the rest of the article, if that makes sense?

Maybe its the fact that the agent blatantly told the guy there was an anomaly, which seems really weird and out of character for an agent to say, when their whole point is to divert attention away from possible anomalous phenomena, and even with the context of the conversation in mind, and the use of targeted amnestics, it just seems so out of place that it ruined the tone for me.

Other than that, it's a compact article with a good story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did consider it, honestly! I added some details to addendum 4 to make the order make more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still would've preferred the addenda to be 1, 3, 4, 2 but +1 anyways, still good.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
There's quite a few people to thank for helping review this. BlueJones, LordOfLaugh, and others who I'm totally spacing on right now.

This is firmly in the realm of the alchemy canon.

Thank you, and I hope you enjoy it!
